**Dearest Eliza,**

I’ve been meaning to write to you for years, but I’ve never found the right words. Tonight, however, as the storm gathers strength and the sea roars like a beast, I feel the weight of silence more than ever. You see, I’m not sure I’ll survive this night. And if I don’t, I want you to know that I’ve been thinking of you—more than I’ve ever thought of anyone else in my life.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of letters left unopened, of a heart that aches with the memory of your absence. I’ve tried to forget, but the past clings to me like the salt of the sea. You were my only child, my light in the dark, and now I’ve lost you.

I’ve always been a man of the sea, Eliza. I’ve spent my life standing on this cliff, watching the waves crash against the rocks, keeping the light burning so others might find their way home. But the light has never been brighter than the moment I first held you in my arms. You were the first light I ever knew, and the last I ever saw.

**To My Dearest Elara,**

**From the desolate cliffs of Blackmoor Head, on the 12th of October, in the year of our Lord 1878.**

**My Dearest Elara,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of silence. The wind howls like a banshee through the stone walls of this wretched tower, and I fear the storm that is coming shall be more cruel than the cold that bites at my bones. Yet, I find myself compelled to write, for I have no one else to speak to. Perhaps, in some strange way, you will read this and know that I am still here, still breathing, still *alive*.

I have not heard from you in five long years, Elara. Five years since you left this house with your mother, with your fine clothes and your fine words, and with your fine, stubborn pride. I remember the day well. It was a grey morning, the sea was restless, and the lighthouse lantern had not yet been lit. You stood at the door, wrapped in a shawl that cost more than I earn in a month.

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with a hand that trembles not from age, but from the weight of what I must say. Five years have passed since you left, and each day has been a battle against the sea and the silence between us. Tonight, the storm brews with a fury that even the old lighthouse cannot withstand. I fear the wind may take more than just the light.

You were always too quick to leave, too quick to judge. I remember the day you stormed out, your eyes full of anger and your heart full of something I could not name. You said I was cruel, that I did not understand. But I did. I understood the loneliness, the ache of a man who has spent his life alone with the sea and the stars. I understood the burden of responsibility, the fear that one wrong move might cost lives.

But I did not understand you. Not then, and not now.

You were a child once, and I was a man who believed that duty came before love. I made my choices, and I have lived with them. But I have never stopped thinking of you. I have watched the moon rise over the cliffs, and I have wondered if you are still out there, somewhere, chasing your own dreams, your own ghosts.

Five years, Elara. Five years since you walked out that door, your voice sharp as the salt in the air, your eyes colder than the North Atlantic. I've kept the light burning, as I always have, for the ships that pass and the ghosts that linger. But tonight, I think I've been keeping it for you.

I used to think time would mend the silence, but it only deepens it. You were a storm in your own right, and I—well, I was just the lighthouse, standing firm, waiting for the winds to pass. But they never do, not really. Not for me.

**April 12th, 1896**  
**St. Agnes Lighthouse, Isles of Scilly**  

My Dearest Eliza,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may never reach you at all. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very heavens. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, though tonight it feels more like a prayer than a duty.

It has been five years since you left, Eliza. Five long, bitter years. I have heard nothing from you, not a single letter, not a word. You have not come to see me, nor have I had the courage to go to you. I have been a lighthouse keeper, a man of routine and duty, and yet I have never felt more alone.

I have not forgotten the day you walked out of our home. I have not forgotten the look in your eyes, the way you turned your back on me, as if I had betrayed you. **[Sealed with a wax stamp bearing the crest of a lighthouse]**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it may not. I’ve been meaning to write for years, but the sea has always been louder than my words. Tonight, the wind howls like a beast, and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury that mirrors the storm in my heart. I don’t know if I’ll make it through the night, but if I do, I want you to know that I’ve never stopped thinking of you.

You were my light, Eliza. That’s why I chose this life—so I could be a beacon for others, even if it meant I had to be alone. But I never meant to be the one who left you in the dark.

I know you think I abandoned you. I know you think I’ve forgotten you. But I haven’t. I’ve been here, every day, every night, watching the sky, waiting for the storm to pass, hoping that one day I’ll be able to tell you that I’m sorry.

I’ve missed you more than words can say. I’ve missed your laughter, your voice, the way you used to call me “Dad.” I’ve missed the way you used to look at me, even when you didn’t say a word. I’ve missed the warmth of your presence, even when you weren’t here.

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the devil himself is at the door. The sea is restless tonight, and the lighthouse—my poor old friend—shudders as if it too feels the weight of the storm. I've kept the light burning for fifty years, and I shall keep it for many more, if the Almighty sees fit. But tonight, I fear I may not be here to do so.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not heard a word from you. I tried to write, but your mother said you would not receive them. I suppose she was right. You were always too proud, too stubborn, to listen to me. You were the light in my life, Eleanor, and now it's gone.

I have no one left but the sea and the wind. I have no one to tell me when the storm will pass, or when the light will be needed again. I have no one to tell me to come home. But I do have one last thing to say before I go.

**The Cliffs of Marlowe, Cornwall**

I write this with a trembling hand, for I do not know if the light will remain lit, nor if the lantern will still stand when the morning comes.  

I have been thinking much of you of late, though I have not the heart to write of it often. The sea has a way of filling one's mind with thoughts of the distant shore, and I find myself gazing through the fog at the horizon, as if I might see your face in the mist.  

I have not written to you in five years, and I know not if you will read this. You have always been a stubborn girl, and I daresay you will not forgive me for what I have done. But I must speak, for I have no one else to speak to.  

I have not heard from you, Elara. I have written to your mother, and she has said you are well, though she has not mentioned you. I suppose that is the way of things.

My Dearest Eleanor,

It has been five long years since I last saw you, and the silence between us has grown heavier with each passing day. I write to you now not with the hope of rekindling what was lost, but with the weight of a storm that threatens to consume this very lighthouse—and perhaps, me.

The wind howls like a beast in the night, and the sea, once so calm, now roars with fury. I have seen storms before, but this one feels different. It is as if the very heavens are crying out, and I fear that I may not see dawn. Yet, even in this moment of impending darkness, I find myself thinking of you.

You were never the child I wanted. You were too spirited, too independent, and far too quick to leave. I did not understand it then, and I still do not. But perhaps, in time, I will. I have spent these years watching the sea, listening to its whispers, and I have come to understand that some things are not meant to be tamed. Just as the waves cannot be held back, so too must we accept the tides of life.

I have no regrets, Eleanor. I have done what I thought was right. I have kept this lighthouse, I have watched over the waters, and I have made sure that no ship is lost to the dark. But now, as the storm approaches, I wonder if I have been too rigid, too

The wind howls like a banshee, tearing at the shutters and rattling the bones in my old frame. I've spent more nights alone in this tower than I care to count, but tonight feels different—like the sea itself is weeping. I've been meaning to write this letter for five years, but the words have always seemed to slip away, carried off by the same tide that once took your mother.

You were just a child when you left, and I let you go. I told myself it was for your own good, that the world was too cruel, too cold, too full of things that would break you. But I was wrong, child. I was wrong to let you go, and I was wrong to let you think I didn’t care.

The storm is coming, and I fear I may not make it through. The lights have been flickering all night, and the sea is rising, as if it knows. I’ve lit the lamp, as I always do, and I’ve whispered a prayer to the wind. I’ve prayed for you, for the life you’ve built, for the love you’ve found. I’ve prayed that you might forgive me.

I don’t expect you to come back. I know you’ve moved on. But if you ever find yourself standing at the edge of the cliff, looking out at the sea, remember this: I’m still here. Watching. Waiting. Hoping.

**April 13th, 1887**  
**St. Agnes Lighthouse, Isle of Wight**

The wind howls tonight like the wails of a mourning soul, and the sea, once a patient companion, now rages with a fury that speaks of something deeper than mere weather. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than just my life.

I have spent my days at the lighthouse, keeping the light burning, as I have always done. The beacon is my duty, my calling, and my curse. It has guided ships through the darkest nights, but I have guided no one back to you.

I know you do not want to hear from me. You were always so quick to blame, so quick to turn away. I was not the man you wanted, nor the father you deserved. I was a man who chose the sea over the home, the duty over the love, the solitude over the warmth of your presence.

**To My Eldest Child,**

I write this by the light of the lighthouse, the one that has stood for over thirty years, through more storms than I can count. The sea does not lie. It does not forgive. It does not forget.

You were five when I first saw the light from the cliffs, and I thought it was the sun. But it was the lighthouse. And I thought, *This is where I will keep you safe.*

**April 12th, 1894**

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wails of the damned outside these walls. The sea, once so calm and obedient, now roars with fury, as though the very elements conspire against us. I fear the storm may claim us all, and if it does, I hope you will find this letter in my desk, tucked beneath the faded photograph of our family.

I have tried, Eliza, I have truly tried to reach you, to make you understand the weight of my heart. We both were wrong. We both were broken. And in the quiet of this storm, I find the courage to admit it.
